passages.explanation: The high priest asks Stephen if the charges are true. Stephen begins his defence — but it's not really a defence. It's a sweeping retelling of Israel's history that builds toward a devastating conclusion. He starts with Abraham: God appeared to him in Mesopotamia before he even reached Haran, told him to leave his country and go to a land God would show him. God promised the land to Abraham's descendants even though Abraham never owned any of it. God also predicted 400 years of slavery and oppression before deliverance. He gave Abraham the covenant of circumcision.

Stephen moves to Joseph: the patriarchs were jealous of their brother and sold him into Egypt. But God was with Joseph, rescued him from all his troubles, and gave him favour before Pharaoh. When famine struck, Jacob sent the patriarchs to Egypt for grain. On the second visit, Joseph revealed himself, and the whole family — 75 people — moved to Egypt. The pattern Stephen is establishing is clear: God's chosen people repeatedly reject the people God sends to save them.

Stephen covers Moses' early life in detail. Born during the period of oppression, he was beautiful before God, raised in Pharaoh's household, and educated in all the wisdom of Egypt. At 40, he visited his own people and killed an Egyptian who was mistreating a Hebrew. He assumed his people would understand that God was using him to rescue them — but they didn't. The next day, a Hebrew pushed him away, saying 'who made you a ruler and judge over us?' Moses fled to Midian, where he lived as a foreigner for 40 years. The rejection pattern continues.

After 40 years in the wilderness, an angel appears to Moses in the burning bush near Mount Sinai. God identifies himself as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Moses trembles and can't look. God tells him to remove his sandals — the place is holy ground. God has seen his people's suffering in Egypt and has come to deliver them. He sends Moses back. Stephen emphasises the irony: this is the same Moses they rejected, saying 'who made you a ruler?' — God sent him as both ruler and deliverer.

This is the same Moses who told Israel that God would raise up a prophet like him from among their own people. Moses received living oracles on Mount Sinai to pass on. But — and this is Stephen's point sharpening — their ancestors refused to obey Moses. They pushed him away and in their hearts turned back to Egypt. They told Aaron to make gods to lead them. They made a golden calf, offered sacrifices to an idol, and celebrated what their own hands had made. God turned away and gave them over to worship the stars. Stephen quotes the prophet Amos to drive the point home.

Stephen shifts to the tabernacle and temple. Their ancestors had the tabernacle of testimony in the wilderness, made exactly as God instructed Moses. Joshua brought it into the promised land. Eventually David wanted to build a permanent dwelling for God, and Solomon built the temple. But — and this is where Stephen hits hardest — the Most High doesn't live in houses made by human hands. He quotes Isaiah: heaven is God's throne, earth is his footstool. What kind of house could you build for him? The charge against Stephen was that he spoke against the temple. His response: you're the ones who reduced God to a building.

Stephen drops all diplomatic pretence. You stiff-necked people, uncircumcised in heart and ears — you always resist the Holy Spirit, just like your ancestors did. Which prophet didn't your ancestors persecute? They killed those who predicted the coming of the Righteous One, and now you have betrayed and murdered him yourselves. You received the law through angels but didn't keep it. The entire speech has been building to this: the pattern of Israel's history is rejecting the people God sends, and they've just done it again with Jesus.

The council is furious — literally grinding their teeth. But Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, looks up to heaven and sees the glory of God with Jesus standing at the right hand of God. He tells them what he sees. They cover their ears, rush at him with one purpose, drag him out of the city, and begin stoning him. The witnesses lay their coats at the feet of a young man named Saul. This is the first mention of the man who will become the apostle Paul.

As they're stoning him, Stephen prays — and his prayers echo Jesus on the cross. 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.' Then, falling to his knees, he cries out with a loud voice: 'Lord, don't hold this sin against them.' Having said this, he falls asleep. Stephen becomes the first Christian martyr, and he dies forgiving his killers, just as his Lord did.
